title: Install user-scoped capped kernel spec for VS Code / any Jupyter client
description:
Summary
-------
Add a Jupyter kernel spec that any client (VS Code primarily, also
JupyterLab/classic Notebook/nbconvert) can pick from its kernel
dropdown. Selecting it spawns Python inside a systemd-run --user
--scope cgroup with the same 48 GiB / 4 GiB caps as wz1.1's
launch_jupyter.sh.

The cap travels with the kernel choice, not the launch method, so
no special launch command or wrapped server is required. Anna runs
notebooks in VS Code; this gives her the wz1.1 safety net by simply
picking "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)" from the kernel dropdown.

Out of scope: deprecating launch_jupyter.sh (still useful for the
"JupyterLab in a browser" case). The two coexist.

Pre-flight (IMPORTANT)
----------------------
Branch this work from origin/main, NOT from feature/replication-4zo.
The wz1.1 launcher (launch_jupyter.sh) and its test pattern
(tests/test_launch_jupyter_sh.py) — which this task models — only
exist on main. Working from the older branch will cause the
"read for context" steps to fail.

Files to create
---------------
- replication/scripts/launch_kernel.sh
    The argv target of the kernel spec. Resolves the venv's python
    relative to ${BASH_SOURCE[0]} so it's portable across repo
    locations (no hardcoded /home/anna paths). Mirrors
    launch_jupyter.sh structure: cgroup-v2 guard, env-var overrides
    (PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX, PH_KERNEL_SWAP_MAX), exec systemd-run.

- replication/scripts/install_kernel_spec.py
    Writes ~/.local/share/jupyter/kernels/ph-project-capped/kernel.json
    with argv pointing at launch_kernel.sh (absolute path, resolved at
    install time) and display_name="Python 3 (ph-project, capped)".
    Idempotent: re-running overwrites with the same content.

- tests/test_launch_kernel_sh.py
    Hermetic tests analogous to tests/test_launch_jupyter_sh.py:
    bash -n syntax check, cgroup-v2 guard fires on shimmed v1, guard
    passes on real v2, default and custom MemoryMax/MemorySwapMax via
    PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX/PH_KERNEL_SWAP_MAX, "$@" forwarding to
    ipykernel_launcher, venv python is correctly resolved.

    Note on test mechanics (per plan review): the PATH-prepend shim
    trick used in test_launch_jupyter_sh.py only intercepts commands
    looked up via PATH (stat, systemd-run). It will NOT intercept the
    launcher's $VENV_PYTHON because that is an ABSOLUTE path computed
    at runtime. To test venv resolution / the "venv python missing"
    failure case, copy launch_kernel.sh into a tmp directory tree
    that has (or lacks) a fake .venv/bin/python alongside it, then
    run the copy. systemd-run is still PATH-shimmed so no real scope
    is created.

- tests/test_install_kernel_spec.py
    Override Path.home / pass tmp paths to install() so HOME doesn't
    get touched. Verify install creates a valid kernel.json with
    correct argv pointing at the launcher and the expected
    display_name; verify idempotency (second install overwrites with
    identical content).

Files to modify
---------------
- replication/README.md
    Two edits:
    a) Add a "Capped kernel for VS Code / Jupyter" subsection in the
       "Running notebooks" area. Steps:
         1. python replication/scripts/install_kernel_spec.py
         2. In VS Code: Select Kernel -> "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)"
         3. (Same dropdown in JupyterLab.)
       Mention the env-var overrides and the cgroup-v2 requirement.
    b) Update the Layout tree (currently lists download_webtext.sh,
       prepare_csv.py, patch_notebook.py, predict_threshold_only.py,
       launch_jupyter.sh) to add launch_kernel.sh and
       install_kernel_spec.py.

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- replication/scripts/launch_jupyter.sh
    The wz1.1 wrapper. launch_kernel.sh follows the same structure
    (guard, env vars, exec) but with a different exec target.

- tests/test_launch_jupyter_sh.py
    The shim pattern (stat shim, systemd-run shim) to copy for the
    new launcher tests. NOTE the limitation called out above — the
    PATH-shim trick doesn't cover the absolute venv python path.

- /home/anna/ph-project/.venv/share/jupyter/kernels/python3/kernel.json
    The default Python 3 kernel spec, for reference on the schema
    (argv, display_name, language, metadata.debugger).

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Write replication/scripts/launch_kernel.sh:

    #!/usr/bin/env bash
    set -euo pipefail

    SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
    PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../.." && pwd)"
    VENV_PYTHON="$PROJECT_ROOT/.venv/bin/python"

    # cgroup v2 guard — same rationale as launch_jupyter.sh
    if [ "$(stat -fc %T /sys/fs/cgroup 2>/dev/null)" != "cgroup2fs" ]; then
      echo "ERROR: /sys/fs/cgroup is not cgroup2fs; this kernel launcher requires cgroup v2." >&2
      exit 1
    fi

    if [ ! -x "$VENV_PYTHON" ]; then
      echo "ERROR: venv python not found at $VENV_PYTHON" >&2
      exit 1
    fi

    MEM_MAX="${PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX:-48G}"
    SWAP_MAX="${PH_KERNEL_SWAP_MAX:-4G}"

    exec systemd-run --user --scope --quiet \
      -p "MemoryMax=${MEM_MAX}" \
      -p "MemorySwapMax=${SWAP_MAX}" \
      "$VENV_PYTHON" -m ipykernel_launcher "$@"

   chmod +x the file.

2. Write replication/scripts/install_kernel_spec.py:

    """Install ph-project-capped Jupyter kernel spec to user dir."""
    import json
    import os
    import sys
    from pathlib import Path

    REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]
    LAUNCHER = REPO_ROOT / "replication" / "scripts" / "launch_kernel.sh"
    KERNEL_DIR = Path.home() / ".local" / "share" / "jupyter" / "kernels" / "ph-project-capped"

    def install(target_dir: Path = KERNEL_DIR, launcher: Path = LAUNCHER) -> Path:
        if not launcher.exists():
            sys.exit(f"Launcher not found: {launcher}")
        if not os.access(launcher, os.X_OK):
            sys.exit(f"Launcher not executable: {launcher}")
        target_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        spec = {
            "argv": [str(launcher), "-f", "{connection_file}"],
            "display_name": "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)",
            "language": "python",
        }
        target = target_dir / "kernel.json"
        target.write_text(json.dumps(spec, indent=2) + "\n")
        return target

    if __name__ == "__main__":
        path = install()
        print(f"Installed kernel spec at {path}")

   Factor install() out so tests can pass override paths without
   monkey-patching globals.

3. Write tests/test_launch_kernel_sh.py modeled on
   test_launch_jupyter_sh.py. Reuse the same shim pattern (temp dir
   prepended to PATH with stat / systemd-run shims). For the
   venv-resolution and venv-missing cases, copy launch_kernel.sh into
   a tmp tree with (or without) a fake .venv/bin/python and run the
   copy. Cover:
     - script exists and is executable
     - bash -n passes
     - cgroup v2 guard fires on shimmed v1, message contains "cgroup2fs"
     - cgroup v2 guard passes on real v2 (this host)
     - default MemoryMax=48G and MemorySwapMax=4G in systemd-run argv
     - PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX / PH_KERNEL_SWAP_MAX overrides apply
     - extra args are forwarded after ipykernel_launcher
     - venv python missing -> exit 1 with clear error
     - venv python present at expected location -> exec proceeds

4. Write tests/test_install_kernel_spec.py. Use tmp_path for the
   target dir; pass override args to install() so HOME doesn't get
   touched. Cover:
     - kernel.json is valid JSON
     - argv has shape [<launcher>, "-f", "{connection_file}"]
     - display_name matches "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)"
     - language == "python"
     - argv[0] points at an existing executable file
     - second install is idempotent (file content identical)
     - install fails fast if launcher does not exist
     - install fails fast if launcher exists but is not executable

5. Update replication/README.md:
   a) Add the new subsection under "Running notebooks":

      ### Capped kernel for VS Code / Jupyter

      Install once:

          python replication/scripts/install_kernel_spec.py

      Then in VS Code, click the kernel picker (top right of any
      notebook) and choose **Python 3 (ph-project, capped)**. The
      same option appears in JupyterLab's kernel dropdown.

      Each kernel start spawns inside a systemd-run --user --scope
      cgroup with MemoryMax=48G and MemorySwapMax=4G. Override via
      PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX / PH_KERNEL_SWAP_MAX env vars. Requires
      cgroup v2 (the launcher refuses to run on cgroup v1).

   b) Update the Layout tree (currently after the "Notebook edits
      needed" section) to list launch_kernel.sh and
      install_kernel_spec.py alongside the existing scripts.

6. Manual verification (note in PR test plan, not automated):
     - python replication/scripts/install_kernel_spec.py
     - jupyter kernelspec list -> confirm "ph-project-capped" appears
     - In VS Code, Select Kernel -> "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)"
       opens a kernel
     - In a cell: import os; os.system("systemctl --user status | grep MemoryMax")
       should show MemoryMax=48G on the scope unit

Testing notes
-------------
This is a launch path with the same risk profile as wz1.1 (a runaway
kernel can take down north). The shim-based tests catch regressions
in the script logic; live OOM verification is the user's job in the
PR test plan, same as wz1.1.

The tests for both scripts must NOT touch the real
~/.local/share/jupyter/kernels directory — use tmp_path.

VS Code kernel discovery: ~/.local/share/jupyter/kernels/ is the
standard user-scoped kernel dir, picked up by jupyter_client's
KernelSpecManager which the VS Code Jupyter extension delegates to.
This is verified to work on north.

Acceptance
----------
- launch_kernel.sh exists, is executable, bash -n clean.
- install_kernel_spec.py installs the spec to user kernels dir.
- jupyter kernelspec list shows "ph-project-capped" after install.
- VS Code kernel picker shows "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)".
- A kernel started via that spec runs inside a systemd-run scope
  with MemoryMax=48G visible via systemctl --user status.
- All tests pass.
- README documents install + selection in both VS Code and JupyterLab,
  and the Layout tree lists the two new files.

title: Replication: patch and run features_prediction.ipynb
description:
The prediction notebook (replication/notebooks/features_prediction.ipynb)
concatenates features from THREE families: threshold (Section 4.1),
barcode (Section 4.2), and template/distance-to-pattern (Section 4.3).
Loading any of barcode_train_file or templ_train_file will fail until
ph-project-nmh (ripserplusplus) is resolved, since those features are
computed in features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb.

INTERIM (2026-04-30): added replication/scripts/predict_threshold_only.py
as a smaller-scope substitute. It trains logistic regression on threshold
features alone and reports accuracy + matthews corrcoef. This unblocks
producing *a* classifier number now without waiting on ripserplusplus.

What this issue still tracks (do NOT close on the script-only path):

Files to edit when ripser/template features become available:
- replication/notebooks/features_prediction.ipynb (cells 5, 8, 12-14, 16)

Edits expected:
1. Setup cell at top with sys.path additions for reference/ and
   replication/ (same as threshold notebook).
2. train_subset/test_subset: "test_5k" -> "train" / "test".
3. input_dir: "./small_gpt_web/" -> "../data/processed/".
4. Add features_dir = "../outputs/" and replace
   `input_dir + "features/"` -> `features_dir + "features/"` in the
   feature-file path constructions (cell 5 and around old_f_/ripser_/templ_).
5. Verify no batch_encode_plus/pad_to_max_length usage (cursory grep
   shows none, but full pass needed).

Verification once features all exist: running should print accuracy and
matthews_corrcoef. With 500/class training data, accuracy will be well
below the paper's ~85-95% — anything above 50% indicates the pipeline
is wired up correctly.
close_reason: Merged in PR #6 (full notebook patches + dispatcher + 30 tests). Anna's interim path predict_threshold_only.py landed via PR #5. End-to-end notebook execution still gated on barcode notebook outputs — see ph-project-qrs.

title: Tighten env-isolation in launcher shim tests
description: Reviewer flagged: tests/test_launch_kernel_sh.py and tests/test_launch_jupyter_sh.py both use a _run_script helper that builds env from os.environ.copy() then .update(env_override). update() overwrites but never deletes, so popping a var from a dict copy in the test does not actually unset it for the subprocess. Default-value tests (test_default_mem_max_*) silently pass for the wrong reason if the developer has PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX (or JUPYTER_MEM_MAX) exported in their shell. Fix: extend _run_script to accept an unset_keys list and call env.pop(k, None) for each, then update test_default_* callers to pass unset_keys=["PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX"] etc. Apply to both test files.

title: Kushnareva replication: run pipeline as binary classifier on WebText/GPT-2
description:
Replicate the original Kushnareva 2021 pipeline (binary human/machine text
classification) before adapting to cross-linguistic comparison. Pedagogical
goal: build intuition for the feature shapes, sanity-check the local stack
(CUDA, ripser, mBERT load), and produce a reference number that any future
port of grab_weights.py can be diffed against.

STATUS (2026-04-30): Smoke test successful. Threshold notebook runs
end-to-end on test split (500 human + 500 machine), producing:
- Attention tensor (1000, 12, 12, 128, 128) float16, range [0, 1],
  mean 1/128 (uniform softmax baseline) — sanity passes.
- Feature tensor (12, 12, 6, 1000, 6) float16, no all-zero slices —
  ripser-zero bug class from the prior tda-project attempt is not
  present here.

Five compat issues found and fixed during the smoke test (recorded in
replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py for repeatability):
1. tokenizer.batch_encode_plus -> tokenizer (transformers 5.x removed).
2. pad_to_max_length=True -> padding="max_length" (renamed kwarg).
3. tokenizer(np.ndarray) requires list[str] (stricter input check).
4. Hardcoded device='cuda:1' -> 'cuda:0' (one-GPU machine).
5. nx.from_numpy_matrix -> nx.from_numpy_array (networkx 3.x removed),
   monkey-patched in setup cell so reference/stats_count.py keeps
   working without edits.

Follow-up work filed:
- ph-project-f93: run threshold notebook on train + valid splits.
- ph-project-3nh: patch and run features_prediction.ipynb.
- ph-project-nmh: handle ripserplusplus dependency (or skip barcode
  notebook for the replication).

reference/ stays frozen throughout — all compat fixes live in
replication/grab_weights_compat.py and the patched setup cell.
close_reason: Smoke test complete: threshold notebook runs end-to-end on test split, sane outputs, no ripser-zero bug. Follow-ups filed as ph-project-f93/3nh/nmh.

title: Investigate why launch_jupyter.sh starts server without token
description:
Symptom
-------
Running replication/scripts/launch_jupyter.sh starts JupyterLab but
the printed URL does NOT include a ?token=... query string. When
VS Code attempts to connect via "Existing Jupyter Server...", it
prompts for a password.

Root cause (confirmed)
----------------------
~/.jupyter/jupyter_lab_config.py contains:

    c.ServerApp.password = 'argon2:$argon2id$v=19$m=10240,t=10,p=8$...'

A password was set (likely via `jupyter server password`) on
2026-02-07. When ServerApp.password is set, Jupyter prefers
password auth and doesn't print a tokenized URL.

Anna doesn't remember the password. Workaround for now: reset via
`jupyter server password`, OR delete the password line entirely
to let Jupyter auto-generate a fresh token per launch (matches
wz1.1's original design).

Acceptance
----------
- Decide between three options:
  (a) reset password to a known value, document where it's stored
      (password manager only — not in repo).
  (b) remove the password line so launch_jupyter.sh prints the
      standard tokenized URL each launch.
  (c) keep current behavior but document in launch_jupyter.sh /
      README that the host uses persistent password auth.
- ph-project-de9 README pivot should reflect whichever choice
  is made.

Out of scope
------------
- Network exposure / remote access hardening — server stays
  localhost-only.
close_reason: Resolved by removing c.ServerApp.password line from ~/.jupyter/jupyter_lab_config.py. Restarting launch_jupyter.sh now auto-generates a fresh token per launch (matches wz1.1's intended design). Verified by Anna 2026-05-02.

title: Phase 1 — KWIC extraction
description: Extract ~200 KWIC sentences per canon term per language from Leipzig Corpora. Three languages, three domains. Outputs feed Phase 3.

title: Canon term lists from literature
description: Build literature-grounded canon term lists per language per domain. Each canon term must trace to a specific publication. Document any deviations from sources.
close_reason: All three subtasks complete and closed (5m7.1 color, 5m7.2 emotion, 5m7.3 kinship). Nine yaml inventories on disk under canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/{color,emotion,kinship}.yaml plus three domain reports and SCHEMA.md. Every term traces to a specific publication; deviations from sources are documented per the project rule. Unblocks ph-project-ssa.3 (term vector extraction).

title: Color domain literature search
description: Berlin and Kay (1969) basic color terms; post-1969 critiques; Russian blue distinction (Davidoff 1999, Winawer 2007, Paramei). Compile authoritative term inventories for English, Russian, Spanish.
close_reason: Complete. canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/color.yaml all on disk: EN 11 BCTs (Berlin & Kay 1969 Stage VII), RU 12 BCTs (Berlin & Kay + Davidoff 1999 goluboj split), ES 11 BCTs with documented variants. canon-terms/color_report.md provides the interpretive framing.

title: Emotion domain literature search
description: Wierzbicka NSM primes; Russian тоска literature (Wierzbicka 1992, Apresjan); Spanish duende and affective vocabulary. Compile authoritative term inventories for English, Russian, Spanish.
close_reason: Complete. canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/emotion.yaml all on disk: 18 EN, 19 RU, 22 ES terms. Ekman 1992 spine + Wierzbicka 1999 NSM augmentations + culture-specific terms (RU тоска/грусть/печаль/жалость/совесть/тревога; ES duende/cariño/añoranza/ilusión). canon-terms/emotion_report.md frames the analytical purpose.

title: Kinship domain literature search
description: Murdock (1949) Social Structure; Greenberg cross-linguistic kinship typology; Kronenfeld. Compile authoritative term inventories for English, Russian, Spanish.
close_reason: Complete. canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/kinship.yaml all on disk. Anchored in Murdock 1949 typology + Friedrich 1964 for Russian affinal terms + Greenberg 1966 universals + Kronenfeld 1996 for step-relations. RU includes the lexicalized husband-side/wife-side affinal distinctions (свёкор/тесть, свекровь/тёща, деверь/шурин, золовка/свояченица) that EN collapses. canon-terms/kinship_report.md explains the typological signal.

title: Replication: rework attention persistence to avoid filling disk
description:
ph-project-f93 (running threshold notebook on train + valid splits)
filled the host disk to 100% before completing — to the point where
bash itself stopped initializing. Each subset persists ~4.7GB of
attention tensors (1000 samples × 12 layers × 12 heads × 128² ×
float16) to replication/outputs/attentions/, with three subsets
intended (test already done, train and valid pending).

Two viable rework directions:

A. Stream — modify the threshold pipeline so attention tensors are
   computed on the fly per sample (or per small batch), features
   are extracted, and attention is discarded immediately. Disk only
   needs to hold the (much smaller) feature arrays.

B. Persist with cleanup — keep the persistence pattern from the
   Kushnareva original but add explicit cleanup steps that delete
   per-subset attention files once features are extracted, and
   document the disk budget loudly in the notebook.

Option A is the bigger refactor but cleaner long-term; Option B is
the smaller change. Pick based on how invasive A would be vs. how
many more notebooks (barcode, templates) will hit the same constraint.

Discovered while ph-project-f93 was running. f93 itself is now blocked
on this — re-running the threshold notebook for train + valid will hit
the same wall unless the persistence pattern changes.

Note: 9.4GB of attention data was successfully written for the train
subset before disk filled. Anna manually deleted these to recover.

ARTIFACT TO SALVAGE:
The killed f93 implementer wrote a useful but uncommitted test file:
  /home/anna/ph-project-replication-f93/tests/test_replication_features.py
It parametrizes over train/valid/test and verifies feature .npy
shape (12,12,6,1000,6). When f93 resumes (after this issue lands),
move that file into tests/ as a quality gate. Note it currently
asserts existence so will fail until all three splits have run —
either gate the existence checks behind a pytest.mark.skipif on
file presence, or only enable after the run completes.

title: Phase 4 — comparison and prediction tests
description: Statistical comparison of feature profiles cross-linguistically. Test the four pre-registered predictions. Mann-Whitney U, permutation, bottleneck/Wasserstein distances.

title: wz1.2 OLD_BARCODE_LOOP pattern is brittle — splits=2 hardcoded
description:
Discovered while setting up wz1.3 verification.

OLD_BARCODE_LOOP in patch_notebook.py (line 241) hardcodes the substring
"number_of_splits = 2\n" as part of the OLD pattern. If anyone bumps
that constant to a different value before running the wz1.2 patch,
the OLD substring no longer matches the live notebook and the patch
silently no-ops on cell 29 (only cell 28 lands).

This actually happened during wz1.3 setup: the parallel
feature/replication-4zo branch had ph-project-xyo commits that bumped
splits 2->20 in the local notebook. When we tried to apply the wz1.2
patch to that local notebook, only the cell-28 patch landed.

Workaround applied for wz1.3: directly swapped cell 29 source to
NEW_BARCODE_LOOP via nbformat. The patch script itself was not updated.

Fix options:
  a) Switch OLD_BARCODE_LOOP from str.replace to re.sub with a more
     permissive regex (allow any int for splits).
  b) Split cell-29 into two patches: normalise splits to a known value
     first, then OLD->NEW.

Either way, add a test that the patch survives a notebook where
splits has been bumped to 20.

title: wz1.2 review-fix: tighten ripser mmap tests to actually guard regression
description:
Discovered during wz1.2 test-quality review. Two non-trivial gaps in the
new tests that would let a regression slip through silently.

Issue 1 (tests/test_ripser_mmap.py)
-----------------------------------
The parent-RSS test fixture is shape (8, 2, 2, 8, 8) float16 = ~4 KB on
disk. Threshold is 10 MB. Reviewer confirmed experimentally: even with
the OLD broken behavior (parent np.load(allow_pickle=True) + 20
fancy-index copies), the parent RSS delta is ~0.07 MB — two orders of
magnitude below the threshold. Test passes with the broken behavior on
this fixture, so it provides zero regression protection.

Fix: bump fixture to ~25 MB on disk (shape (50_000, 2, 2, 8, 8) float16
suffices and runs in under a second). Confirm experimentally that the
OLD pre-load pattern exceeds the 10 MB threshold while the new mmap
path stays under.

Issue 2 (tests/test_patch_notebook.py)
--------------------------------------
test_barcode_loop_passes_filename_and_indices checks the substring
"filename, indices,". This passes even if someone wrote
(filename, indices, ntokens, ...) instead of the required
(filename, indices, ntokens[indices], ...).

Fix: add an assertion that "ntokens[indices]" appears in the loop cell
source.

Acceptance
----------
- Parent-RSS test fails on the OLD pattern, passes on the NEW pattern.
- test_barcode_loop_passes_filename_and_indices asserts ntokens slicing.
- Full test suite remains green.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 7d315d6 (part of wz1.2 PR)

title: VS Code 2025.x Jupyter extension does not surface shell-script kernel specs
description:
Symptom
-------
The kernel spec installed by ph-project-1za
(~/.local/share/jupyter/kernels/ph-project-capped/kernel.json) is
correctly registered — `jupyter kernelspec list` shows it, and the
launcher actually starts an ipykernel when invoked directly. But
VS Code's notebook kernel picker does NOT show "Python 3
(ph-project, capped)" anywhere. The picker only surfaces Python
interpreters (venvs / conda envs); the "Jupyter Kernel..." path
that used to expose registered kernel specs has been removed.

Anna's installed extensions:
  ms-toolsai.jupyter@2025.9.1
  ms-python.python@2026.4.0
  ms-python.vscode-python-envs@1.28.0

Known related issues
--------------------
- microsoft/vscode-jupyter#13796 — Jupyter plugin hides custom
  kernelspecs when executable is not a recognized python.
- microsoft/vscode-python#25554 — env discovery regressions in
  2025.x.

This means the "use the kernel spec from VS Code's picker" UX
documented in the ph-project-1za PR (#13) does not actually work
on Anna's machine today. The kernel spec still works for
JupyterLab-in-browser, classic Notebook, and nbconvert/headless
runs — just not VS Code, which is Anna's primary client.

Workaround in use
-----------------
Use launch_jupyter.sh (wz1.1) to start a JupyterLab server inside
the cgroup, then connect VS Code to that server via "Existing
Jupyter Server..." — kernels spawned by the server inherit the
cgroup. Same cap delivered through a different door, but requires
two terminals (server + VS Code) instead of a single picker click.

Options to consider
-------------------
A. Pivot the README: document the Jupyter-Server route as the
   primary path for VS Code users; keep install_kernel_spec.py
   as the path for JupyterLab/headless users. Lowest-risk, no
   code changes.

B. Wrap launch_kernel.sh in a Python entry point so argv[0] in
   the kernel spec is a real Python interpreter (which VS Code
   recognizes). The Python wrapper would os.execvp into
   launch_kernel.sh. More fragile; defeats some of the
   simplicity of the current design; but restores the
   single-picker-click UX.

C. Wait for Microsoft to restore custom-kernelspec discovery in
   the Jupyter extension. Indefinite timeline.

Files affected
--------------
- replication/README.md — "Capped kernel for VS Code / Jupyter"
  subsection currently overstates VS Code support.
- Possibly add a new wrapper script under replication/scripts/
  if Option B is chosen.

Acceptance
----------
- README accurately reflects what works in current VS Code.
- A single documented path exists for "run a notebook in VS Code
  with the cgroup safety net" — whether that's the server-mode
  workaround (Option A) or a Python-wrapped kernel spec
  (Option B).
- If Option B chosen: tests for the new wrapper, and verification
  that VS Code's picker now shows the capped kernel.
close_reason: Could not reproduce after removing the password from ~/.jupyter/jupyter_lab_config.py (see ph-project-57b). VS Code's kernel picker now shows 'Python 3 (ph-project, capped)', selecting it spawns a kernel inside the expected systemd scope (verified: cgroup path ends in run-rNNNNN.scope, memory.max = 51539607552 bytes = 48 GiB). The original Explore-agent research was overly pessimistic; the actual VS Code Jupyter extension does discover shell-script kernel specs, but its discovery appears to be sensitive to jupyter config state in ways that aren't fully understood. If this regresses, re-open with reproduction details and consider Option B (Python wrapper) from the original issue.

title: Rebase feature/replication-4zo onto main — resolve ripser notebook conflict
description:
Context
-------
feature/replication-4zo has diverged from origin/main. An attempted
straight `git rebase origin/main` (during the ph-project-1za /merge
flow on 2026-05-02) hit a CONFLICT (content) on
replication/notebooks/features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb
and was aborted.

The conflict is non-trivial — both sides intentionally modified the
same notebook for different reasons. Other touched files
(.beads/issues.jsonl, features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb,
patch_notebook.py) auto-merged cleanly.

Conflicting commits
-------------------
On the branch:
  c613533 ph-project-xyo: extend patch_notebook.py for ripser/template notebook
          - re-patched the ripser notebook via the extended patcher
  8803339 ph-project-xyo: bump number_of_splits to 20 in ripser notebook
          - parameter bump in the same notebook

On main:
  ad60a62 ph-project-wz1.2: refactor ripser cell 29 — fork+mmap, fix OOM (#12)
          - rewrote cell 29 to use fork+mmap to fix an OOM in the
            ripser computation

Both changes are wanted: Anna's branch needs the patcher-driven
notebook regeneration AND the wz1.2 OOM fix. They overlap in cell
content, not in intent.

Approach
--------
1. Spawn the rebase subagent with full context (it will read both
   sides of the conflict, including the parent commits, and propose
   a reconciliation):

     ROLE: Rebase Agent (Conflict Resolution)
     SKILL: Read and follow .claude/skills/rebase/SKILL.md

     SOURCE: feature/replication-4zo
     TARGET: origin/main
     WORKTREE: <create a fresh worktree, do NOT do this in the live
                main checkout where Anna has uncommitted work>
     CLEANUP: false

2. The rebase agent should:
   - Inspect cell 29 of the ripser notebook on both sides
   - Identify whether the patcher (c613533) or the wz1.2 refactor
     (ad60a62) should be the source of truth for cell 29 — likely
     the wz1.2 version, with the patcher's other-cell changes
     reapplied on top
   - Re-run the patcher on the post-rebase notebook if appropriate,
     so the patcher remains the source of truth
   - Propose the resolved notebook for Anna's sign-off, NOT
     auto-commit

3. Whatever resolution is chosen, document it in the rebase commit
   message so future archaeology is clean.

Pre-flight
----------
- Anna's main checkout (/home/anna/ph-project) currently has
  uncommitted work: rename of features_calculation_by_thresholds.executed.ipynb
  to archive/, staged add of launch_jupyter.sh, modified
  .beads/issues.jsonl, untracked install_kernel_spec.py and
  launch_kernel.sh in replication/scripts/. Do NOT do the rebase
  in that checkout — use a separate worktree.

- After successful rebase, the launch_kernel.sh and
  install_kernel_spec.py untracked files in Anna's main checkout
  will become tracked (they're committed on main). Git should
  recognize them as identical and not flag conflicts. If it does,
  rm the untracked copies first.

Acceptance
----------
- feature/replication-4zo's HEAD is a descendant of origin/main.
- The ripser notebook contains both Anna's patcher-driven changes
  (number_of_splits=20, etc.) AND the wz1.2 cell-29 fork+mmap
  refactor.
- Rebase commit message documents the conflict resolution choice.
- Force-push origin/feature/replication-4zo with the rebased
  history (--force-with-lease).
- Anna's working tree changes survive the rebase (use stash +
  pop pattern, handle .beads/issues.jsonl conflict if it occurs).

Out of scope
------------
- Resolving any other branches' divergence from main.
- Changes to either patcher or wz1.2 logic — only reconciliation.

title: Replication: handle ripserplusplus dependency for barcode notebook
description:
The barcode notebook (replication/notebooks/features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb)
imports `ripserplusplus`, the GPU-accelerated ripser the Kushnareva
paper used. The project venv has CPU `ripser` (0.6.14) but not
`ripserplusplus`. The notebook is currently unrunnable as-is.

Three viable paths (pick one, justify the choice):

A. Install ripserplusplus
   - https://github.com/simonzhang00/ripser-plusplus
   - Requires Pascal+ GPU and a CUDA matching the system.
   - We have CUDA 13.0 and an RTX 5070 Ti (Blackwell). May or may
     not build cleanly — try and see.

B. Write a CPU shim
   - Create replication/ripser_count_compat.py that mirrors
     reference/ripser_count.py's API but calls scikit-learn's
     ripser.ripser() under the hood.
   - Slow but portable.

C. Skip barcode features entirely for the replication
   - The threshold features (Section 4.1) already give a working
     classifier (per ph-project-4zo). Barcode + template features
     (Sections 4.2 + 4.3) are additional richness, not core proof.
   - Document this scope choice and move on.

Recommendation if compute time matters: A first, fall back to C.

Out of scope here: the cross-linguistic mBERT epic (ph-project-mwk).
Whether the main project uses ripser vs. ripserplusplus is a separate
decision and may be informed by what we learn here.
close_reason: Resolved: ripserplusplus master compiles cleanly with CUDA 13 / RTX 5070 Ti via 'pip install git+https://github.com/simonzhang00/ripser-plusplus.git'. PyPI is stale; upstream master has the cuda12 thrust fix (PR #14, merged 2023). Smoke test on 4-point unit square produces correct H0/H1 barcodes.

title: Phase 2 — fastText baseline
description:
Phase 2 — static embedding baselines (fastText).

Three complementary sub-baselines that together test the project hypothesis
(shape of semantic domains differs across languages) using only static word
embeddings, before introducing mBERT attention:

A. Language-specific descriptive. Per-language fastText CC-300 vectors.
   Pairwise cosine distance matrix per (language, domain). Agglomerative
   clustering with dendrograms and k-means with silhouette sweeps.

B. Language-specific topological. Same distance matrices as A, passed through
   persistent homology (Vietoris-Rips / Ripser). Full Kushnareva-style barcode
   feature set (sum/mean/variance/entropy/threshold counts for H0/H1). Same
   topology pipeline as the main mBERT experiment — this is the primary foil.

C. Cross-lingual aligned. MUSE supervised aligned fastText. Cross-lingual
   cosine distances, joint clustering, joint PH on combined term clouds.
   Tests prediction #2 (emotion divergence > color divergence) descriptively.

All three consume the canon term lists from ph-project-5m7. Output: baseline
figures and feature tables for direct comparison with the main mBERT result
in Phase 4.

Hybrid implementation: computation in importable Python modules under
baselines/ (testable with pytest); narrative analysis in notebooks/ that
import from those modules. Mirrors the Kushnareva reference/ layout
(modules + notebooks).

Multi-word canon terms: take the head word (Anna's call — e.g., "maternal
uncle" → "uncle"; in English the non-head modifier is not salient, the
meaning sits in the head).

title: Download and cache fastText vectors (CC-300 + MUSE aligned)
description:
Summary
-------
Acquire and cache the static embedding vectors used throughout Phase 2 baselines.
Plumbing task only — no analysis yet. Runnable immediately; does not depend on
canon terms.

Vectors to download
-------------------
* fastText CC-300 (Common Crawl, 300-dim, .bin) for English, Russian, Spanish.
  Source: https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/crawl-vectors.html
  Files: cc.en.300.bin, cc.ru.300.bin, cc.es.300.bin
  Used by: sub-baselines A and B (language-specific).

* MUSE supervised aligned fastText for English, Russian, Spanish.
  Source: https://github.com/facebookresearch/MUSE#download (aligned wiki vectors).
  Files: wiki.multi.en.vec, wiki.multi.ru.vec, wiki.multi.es.vec (~600MB each).
  Used by: sub-baseline C (cross-lingual aligned).

PH library pin
--------------
Phase 2 commits to `ripser` (pure Python, CPU). Rationale: simpler install,
CPU is fine for ~30-term distance matrices, and the reference code's use of
`ripserplusplus` is only for the import line — the feature-extraction code
is library-agnostic (operates on already-computed barcodes). Add `ripser` to
requirements.txt if it isn't already confirmed at the expected version.
If Phase 3 mBERT attention PH is slow, file a follow-up to swap in
ripserplusplus (GPU) — that is a Phase 3 decision, not Phase 2.

Files to create
---------------
* baselines/__init__.py — empty package marker
* baselines/vectors.py — module exposing
    load_fasttext(lang: str, kind: str) -> KeyedVectors
  where kind ∈ {"cc", "aligned"}. For "cc", use gensim's
  load_facebook_model(...).wv; for "aligned", use
  KeyedVectors.load_word2vec_format(..., binary=False).
* scripts/download_fasttext.sh — reproducible download script with sha256 checks.
* data/fasttext/README.md — source URLs, file list, sha256s.

Data layout (gitignored)
------------------------
data/fasttext/cc/cc.{en,ru,es}.300.bin
data/fasttext/aligned/wiki.multi.{en,ru,es}.vec

.gitignore update
-----------------
Current .gitignore excludes data/fasttext/ and *.bin/*.npz/*.pt but NOT *.vec.
MUSE files are large .vec text files. Add `*.vec` to .gitignore (belt-and-
suspenders for any stray .vec files outside data/). Confirm existing
data/fasttext/ rule is in place (it is as of 2026-04-16).

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* README.md — pipeline overview
* CLAUDE.md — project conventions (notebook-faithful, no reaching into prior project)
* requirements.txt — gensim and ripser are already listed
* .gitignore — current state

Testing notes
-------------
tests/baselines/test_vectors.py — smoke test that loads each language for each
kind and asserts:
  - vector dim == 300
  - a known in-vocab word (e.g., "cat" / "кот" / "gato") returns a finite,
    non-zero vector

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Verify .gitignore covers data/fasttext/ (it does). Add `*.vec` as a
   belt-and-suspenders rule.
2. Pin PH library: ensure `ripser` is in requirements.txt at a known-good
   version. Do NOT add `ripserplusplus` in Phase 2.
3. Write scripts/download_fasttext.sh; verify sha256 of each file on download.
4. Create baselines/ package with __init__.py and vectors.py.
5. Implement load_fasttext with clear error message if file missing.
6. Write smoke tests.
7. Confirm CUDA / GPU is not required for this step (pure file IO + CPU PH).

title: Term vector extraction module (shared by A/B/C)
description:
Summary
-------
Extract (term × dim) matrices per (language, domain, embedding-kind) from
canon term lists and loaded fastText vectors. Shared input layer for
sub-baselines A, B, and C.

Dependencies
------------
* ssa.1 (vectors downloaded and load_fasttext works)
* ssa.2 (module stubs exist)
* ph-project-5m7.{1,2,3} (canon term lists exist under canon-terms/)

Files to modify
---------------
* baselines/distances.py — implement extract_term_vectors(terms, vectors, strategy)

Behavior
--------
Input: list of canon term strings, a KeyedVectors (or FastText model with
subword support), a strategy flag.

Strategies for multi-word terms (Anna's decision: head-word):
  * "head"  (default) — take the head word per language NP typology:
        EN: head-final in descriptive NPs: "maternal uncle"   → "uncle"     (right)
        RU: head-final with adjective modifier: "двоюродный брат" → "брат" (right)
        ES: head-INITIAL (Spanish NPs are head-initial):
              "tío materno"  → "tío"         (left)
              "hermano mayor" → "hermano"   (left)
        Implementation: per-language head-position lookup
          HEAD_POSITION = {"en": "right", "ru": "right", "es": "left"}
        Override-able via an optional per-call argument.
        If a term has no space, return as-is.
  * "mean"  — average vectors of component words (fallback strategy).
  * "skip"  — drop multi-word terms entirely.

OOV handling:
  * CC .bin (FastText) — subword composition returns a vector for any
    string; no miss. Verify via model.wv.has_index_for or equivalent and
    note that fastText `.vec` (text) files would NOT have this property.
  * MUSE aligned .vec — no subword fallback. Log misses to stderr and to
    a returned boolean mask. Dropped terms do NOT get a zero vector; they
    are excluded and flagged in the mask.

Return: tuple (matrix: np.ndarray of shape (n_found, dim),
               found_mask: np.ndarray[bool] of shape (n_terms,))

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* canon-terms/<lang>/<domain>.yaml — term list format (exists after ph-project-5m7)
* baselines/vectors.py — loader signatures
* gensim fastText docs (if needed for subword API questions)

Testing notes
-------------
tests/baselines/test_distances.py — unit tests for extract_term_vectors:
  * head strategy picks correct word for EN ("maternal uncle" → "uncle")
  * head strategy picks correct word for RU ("двоюродный брат" → "брат")
  * head strategy picks correct word for ES ("tío materno" → "tío")   [CRITICAL:
    confirms the left-head rule for Spanish; if this test fails the lookup
    is inverted]
  * OOV in MUSE-style vectors flags mask=False and drops the row
  * OOV in FastText CC-style vectors returns a finite vector (no drop)
  * output shape matches mask.sum()
Use a small synthetic gensim KeyedVectors fixture so tests don't load real vectors.

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Implement the per-language HEAD_POSITION lookup:
     {"en": "right", "ru": "right", "es": "left"}
2. Implement the three strategies with explicit dispatch on the strategy arg.
3. Log OOV misses; include lang + domain + term in the log line.
4. Unit-test with synthetic KeyedVectors (build with gensim KeyedVectors(dim)
   and add_vectors to inject a tiny vocabulary). Include the three
   language-specific head-word tests above.
5. Smoke-run on one real (lang, domain) pair to confirm end-to-end.

title: Sub-baseline A: descriptive (clustering + dendrograms)
description:
Summary
-------
Compute cosine distance matrices and clustering structure per
(language, domain) on language-specific fastText CC-300 vectors.
This is the human-readable descriptive baseline.

Dependencies
------------
* ssa.3 (term vector extraction)

Files to modify
---------------
* baselines/distances.py — implement cosine_distance_matrix(X) -> np.ndarray
  Use scipy.spatial.distance.pdist(X, metric="cosine") + squareform.
* baselines/clustering.py —
    agglomerative_linkage(D, method="average") -> linkage matrix (scipy.cluster.hierarchy.linkage on a condensed distance matrix)
    kmeans_silhouette_sweep(X, k_range) -> dict mapping k -> silhouette score
* notebooks/baseline_A_descriptive.ipynb — full analysis

Notebook structure
------------------
1. Load canon terms for each (language, domain) — 9 combinations.
2. Load fastText CC-300 for EN, RU, ES via baselines.vectors.load_fasttext.
3. Extract term vector matrices via baselines.distances.extract_term_vectors.
4. Compute per-(lang, domain) cosine distance matrices.
5. Agglomerative clustering + dendrogram per (lang, domain) — 9 dendrograms.
   Use matplotlib; label leaves with the canon term strings.
6. k-means silhouette sweep (k = 2..8) — silhouette plots per (lang, domain).
7. Summary table: cluster count suggested by silhouette peak per (lang, domain).
8. Save figures and summary CSV to results/baseline_A/.

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* canon-terms/*.yaml
* baselines/distances.py, baselines/clustering.py, baselines/vectors.py
* reference/utils.py (style)

Testing notes
-------------
tests/baselines/test_distances.py —
  * test cosine_distance_matrix against scipy.spatial.distance.cosine on
    handpicked pairs
  * symmetric, zero diagonal, bounded [0, 2]
tests/baselines/test_clustering.py —
  * on synthetic 2-cluster data (well-separated), silhouette at k=2 > 0.5
  * agglomerative_linkage returns a (n-1, 4) linkage matrix

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Implement cosine_distance_matrix and the two clustering functions.
2. Write unit tests.
3. Walk through notebook with one (lang, domain) first as a smoke test.
4. Produce all 9 dendrograms + silhouette plots.
5. Save results/baseline_A/{dendrograms/, silhouette/, summary.csv}.

title: Sub-baseline B: topological (PH on static embeddings)
description:
Summary
-------
Compute persistent homology on the language-specific cosine distance
matrices. Reuse the full Kushnareva barcode feature set for direct,
apples-to-apples comparison with the main mBERT result. This is the
primary foil for the whole project.

Dependencies
------------
* ssa.3 (term vector extraction)

PH library
----------
Use `ripser` (CPU) per ssa.1's Phase 2 pin. Do NOT introduce
`ripserplusplus` here.

Files to modify
---------------
* baselines/topology.py —
    rips_barcode(D, max_dim=1) -> dict
        Wraps ripser.ripser on a precomputed distance matrix
        (distance_matrix=True). Validates D is square, symmetric,
        non-negative, finite, non-NaN. Returns
          {0: structured_array, 1: structured_array}
        where each structured array has 'birth' and 'death' float fields.
    barcode_features(barcode) -> dict
        Ports the full reference/ripser_count.py feature set, preserving
        function and feature names:
          h0_s, h0_m, h0_v, h0_e,
          h0_n_d_m_t<t>, h0_n_b_l_t<t>, h0_t_d, h0_t_b,
          plus the h1_* equivalents.

        NAMING CAVEAT — preserved for faithfulness:
        In reference/ripser_count.py, `h{d}_v` is computed by
        `barcode_std` (standard deviation), NOT by variance, despite the
        letter 'v'. Preserve this behavior and the naming exactly. Add
        a one-line docstring to barcode_features noting this:
        "h{d}_v computes std (not variance), matching Kushnareva naming."
        Do NOT silently "fix" the name to match variance — downstream
        comparison requires exact feature-value parity with the main
        mBERT result, which will use the same convention.

        Threshold values: match reference (0.25, 0.5, 0.75) unless a
        domain-specific rationale for changing them is documented.

* notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb — analysis notebook
* tests/baselines/test_topology.py — tests (see below)

Notebook structure
------------------
1. Load term vectors and cosine distance matrices (reuse A's outputs).
2. Compute Ripser barcode per (language, domain).
3. Plot H0 and H1 barcodes side by side across languages per domain.
4. Extract full Kushnareva feature set per (language, domain).
5. Save barcodes (.npz) and feature table (.csv) to results/baseline_B/.
6. Early qualitative look at prediction #1: does Russian color H0 show
   more persistent structure (extra connected component) than EN or ES?

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* reference/ripser_count.py — source of barcode features; preserve naming
  AND the _v-is-std convention
* reference/utils.py — cutoff_matrix style
* baselines/distances.py — where cosine_distance_matrix lives
* ripser API docs

Testing notes
-------------
CRITICAL. The prior tda-project attempt had a silent ripser zero-output
bug (see ph-project-mwk.3). Tests must cover FIVE cases:

  1. Sanity — two well-separated clusters: distance matrix with two
     tight blocks produces exactly 2 persistent H0 bars (one infinite,
     one persistent above threshold).
  2. Input validation — non-square / non-symmetric / NaN / negative
     input raises a clear ValueError BEFORE calling ripser.
  3. Empty barcode — barcode_features on {0: empty, 1: empty} returns
     zeros for all features, never NaN.
  4. Regression — on a fixed synthetic distance matrix, features match
     precomputed expected values (golden numbers in test file).
  5. Degenerate input — all-zero distance matrix (all points coincident)
     produces the documented behavior: a single infinite H0 bar and
     empty H1. NOT a silent empty barcode. This is the exact prior-
     project failure mode.

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Port feature-extraction functions from reference/ripser_count.py into
   baselines/topology.py, preserving names and signatures — INCLUDING
   the _v-is-std convention (document in docstring).
2. Implement rips_barcode with input validation (reject silently-bad input
   BEFORE calling ripser — this is the zero-output-bug firewall).
3. Add all five tests above; include the degenerate-input test as the
   explicit firewall against the prior bug.
4. Walk the notebook end-to-end on one (lang, domain).
5. Run on all 9 (lang, domain) combinations; save artifacts.

title: Sub-baseline C: cross-lingual aligned (MUSE)
description:
Summary
-------
Compute cross-lingual semantic distance using MUSE supervised aligned
fastText vectors. Supports prediction #2 (emotion divergence > color
divergence) descriptively. Formal tests happen in Phase 4.

Dependencies
------------
* ssa.3 (term vector extraction)

Files to modify
---------------
* notebooks/baseline_C_aligned.ipynb — cross-lingual analysis notebook

Notebook structure
------------------
1. Load MUSE aligned vectors for EN, RU, ES via load_fasttext(..., kind="aligned").
2. Extract term matrices per (language, domain) with extract_term_vectors.
   Expect OOV to be higher than CC-300 case — MUSE has no subword fallback.
3. OOV acceptance gate (see below).
4. Cross-lingual cosine distance matrices per domain (rows: EN canon terms,
   columns: RU canon terms; same for EN-ES and RU-ES).
5. Joint distance matrix per domain: all three languages stacked into one
   term × term matrix. Label each row with (lang, term).
6. Joint agglomerative clustering per domain; dendrogram colored by language.
7. Joint Ripser PH per domain — what does the union cloud look like?
   Report H0 count (does Russian cluster separately from EN and ES in color?).
8. Prediction #2 descriptive check: mean cross-lingual cosine distance in
   EMOTION domain vs COLOR domain, per language pair. Table + bar chart.
9. Save to results/baseline_C/.

OOV acceptance gate
-------------------
For each (language, domain), record the OOV rate = (1 - found_mask.mean()).

Behavior by rate (hard-coded, not configurable — this is a correctness gate):
  * OOV ≤ 10%  — proceed. Log the misses in the notebook output.
  * 10% < OOV ≤ 25% — proceed with an explicit warning cell in the
      notebook. Report downstream results with a ⚠ marker. File a
      discovered-from issue linked to ssa.6 flagging the weak coverage
      for canon-terms followup (e.g., "Russian kinship MUSE OOV = 18%").
  * OOV > 25% — STOP. Skip the affected (lang, domain). Record it in
      results/baseline_C/oov_skipped.csv. Downstream joint analysis
      excludes the skipped combinations. ssa.7 (comparison notebook)
      must read this file and mark skipped cells as "insufficient
      coverage — baseline C" in its output tables.

File a discovered-from issue under ssa.6 for any (lang, domain) that
triggers the second or third tier, so term lists can be revisited.

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* baselines/vectors.py, baselines/distances.py, baselines/clustering.py,
  baselines/topology.py
* notebooks/baseline_A_descriptive.ipynb — mirror its plotting patterns
* notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb — mirror its PH plotting patterns

Testing notes
-------------
No new module code under test (notebook reuses existing functions).
Do log MUSE OOV rates prominently per (lang, domain) — they are the
primary reproducibility signal for this sub-baseline.

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Walk the notebook end-to-end on the color domain first as smoke test.
2. Apply the OOV acceptance gate; file discovered-from issues as needed.
3. Produce cross-lingual distance heatmaps, joint dendrograms, joint
   barcodes for all (lang, domain) that pass the gate.
4. Save results/baseline_C/{heatmaps/, dendrograms/, barcodes/, pred2.csv,
   oov_skipped.csv}.

title: wz1.2 review-fix: tighten ripser mmap tests to actually guard regression
description:
Discovered during wz1.2 test-quality review. Two non-trivial gaps in the
new tests that would let a regression slip through silently.

Issue 1 (tests/test_ripser_mmap.py)
-----------------------------------
The parent-RSS test fixture is shape (8, 2, 2, 8, 8) float16 = ~4 KB on
disk. Threshold is 10 MB. Reviewer confirmed experimentally: even with
the OLD broken behavior (parent np.load(allow_pickle=True) + 20
fancy-index copies), the parent RSS delta is ~0.07 MB — two orders of
magnitude below the threshold. The test passes with the broken behavior
on this fixture, so it provides zero regression protection for its
load-bearing invariant.

Fix: bump fixture to ~25 MB on disk (shape (50_000, 2, 2, 8, 8) float16
suffices and still runs in under a second). Confirm experimentally that
the OLD pre-load pattern would now exceed the 10 MB threshold while the
new mmap path stays under.

Issue 2 (tests/test_patch_notebook.py)
--------------------------------------
test_barcode_loop_passes_filename_and_indices checks the substring
"filename, indices,". This passes even if someone wrote
(filename, indices, ntokens, ...) instead of the required
(filename, indices, ntokens[indices], ...). The critical semantic
difference vs. the old code is that ntokens is now sliced; if a future
patch accidentally passed the full array, no test would fail.

Fix: add an assertion that "ntokens[indices]" appears in the loop cell
source.

Acceptance
----------
- Parent-RSS test fails on the OLD pattern, passes on the NEW pattern,
  on a fixture large enough that this is meaningful.
- test_barcode_loop_passes_filename_and_indices asserts ntokens is
  sliced, not passed whole.
- Full test suite remains green.

title: Replication: ripser notebook OOMs north despite splits=20
description:
The ripser/templates notebook (replication/notebooks/features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb)
crashes the kernel on cell 29 (barcode computation) on north (64GB RAM
+ 14GB swap, RTX 5070 Ti 16GB). Confirmed via OOM-style behavior — the
host became unresponsive multiple times.

Diagnosis (corrected from the original wz1 description, which incorrectly
attributed the OOM to 20× fork amplification):

Cell 29 is sequential, not parallel. The loop spawns a Process per split,
then `queue.get()` blocks until the child puts its result, and `p.join()`
waits for the child to fully exit before the next iteration begins. So
only ONE child is alive at any moment. The 20-process pattern exists
because ripser++ has documented GPU/CPU memory leaks across calls — the
authors run each split in a short-lived subprocess so the OS can reclaim
leaked memory at exit. That part is intentional.

The real OOM cause is parent-side bloat:

1. `adj_matricies = np.load(filename, allow_pickle=True)` loads the full
   ~4.7 GB attention tensor (1000 samples × 12 layers × 12 heads × 128 ×
   128, fp16) into the parent kernel.
2. `split_matricies_and_lengths` does
   `[(adj_matricies[ids], ntokens[ids]) for ids in splitted_ids]`.
   Integer-array fancy indexing COPIES, not views — so `splitted` holds
   20 array copies whose total size matches the original (~4.7 GB more).
3. Parent kernel is now ~9.4 GB resident BEFORE any forks.
4. Each fork()ed child inherits all of that COW. The child runs ripser++
   on its slice with documented GPU/RAM leaks, plus its own working set.
5. Cumulatively, with PyTorch CUDA allocator state, tokenizer, BERT
   weights still held by the kernel from the threshold notebook stage,
   etc., the host runs out of physical memory + swap.

Note: the `np.int` compat shim landed in ph-project-xyo and is wired into
the patch_notebook.py setup cell. Not the OOM cause; just an earlier
blocker that's already resolved.

Already-shipped scaffolding (do NOT redo):
- patch_notebook.py has the dispatcher refactor (_SUPPORTED map).
- RIPSER_PATCHES is in place (paths, subset, tokenizer, Pool→gc cleanup,
  splits = 20).
- np.int and networkx-3.x compat shims are in the setup cell.
- ripser_count_compat shim referenced by ph-project-qrs never shipped;
  ph-project-nmh pinned ripserplusplus to upstream master with the
  cuda12 fix instead. We run GPU ripser++ directly.

Fix: switch cell 29 (and helpers in cells 27, 28) to spawn-context
multiprocessing and have children mmap-load only their slice from disk.
Parent never holds the full tensor.

Subtasks under this issue:
- wz1.1: cap kernel memory via systemd-run wrapper (defense-in-depth)
- wz1.2: refactor cell 29 (the actual fix)
- wz1.3: run on test split, verify outputs

Acceptance: ripser notebook completes on test split (1000 samples)
without crashing north. Outputs land in replication/outputs/barcodes/
and replication/outputs/features/ with non-degenerate content. Train
and valid splits are out of scope here — separate follow-up after wz1.3.

Until wz1.3 closes, the threshold-only classifier path
(replication/scripts/predict_threshold_only.py) remains the working
end-to-end pipeline for ph-project-3nh.

title: wz1.1: Cap Jupyter kernel memory via systemd-run wrapper
description:
Summary
-------
Add a Jupyter launch wrapper that caps kernel memory via systemd-run, so
a runaway notebook (especially the ripser barcode loop in cell 29 of
features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb) kills only the kernel
cleanly, not the entire host.

Defense-in-depth before the cell 29 refactor in wz1.2. We've already
crashed north hard several times running this notebook; capping the
cgroup means future iterations are recoverable.

Files to create
---------------
- replication/scripts/launch_jupyter.sh

Files to modify
---------------
- replication/README.md (add a note pointing at the wrapper as the
  recommended way to launch jupyter for this project)

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py (existing scripts conventions)
- replication/README.md (existing structure)

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Create replication/scripts/launch_jupyter.sh with this content:

   #!/usr/bin/env bash
   set -euo pipefail

   # systemd-run -p MemorySwapMax requires cgroup v2. Bail early on
   # systems where /sys/fs/cgroup is not cgroup2fs (would silently
   # miss the swap cap, leaving north exposed).
   if [ "$(stat -fc %T /sys/fs/cgroup 2>/dev/null)" != "cgroup2fs" ]; then
     echo "ERROR: /sys/fs/cgroup is not cgroup2fs; this wrapper requires cgroup v2." >&2
     echo "       Either switch to cgroup v2 or invoke jupyter directly without the cap." >&2
     exit 1
   fi

   # Cap kernel memory so a runaway notebook (e.g. the ripser barcode
   # loop) kills the kernel cleanly without freezing north. 48 GB
   # leaves headroom for the desktop session on a 64 GB box; tune via
   # JUPYTER_MEM_MAX env.
   MEM_MAX="${JUPYTER_MEM_MAX:-48G}"
   SWAP_MAX="${JUPYTER_SWAP_MAX:-4G}"

   exec systemd-run --user --scope --quiet \
     -p "MemoryMax=${MEM_MAX}" \
     -p "MemorySwapMax=${SWAP_MAX}" \
     jupyter lab "$@"

2. chmod +x replication/scripts/launch_jupyter.sh.

3. Add a short "Running notebooks" section to replication/README.md
   pointing at the wrapper. Note the env var overrides
   (JUPYTER_MEM_MAX, JUPYTER_SWAP_MAX) and the cgroup-v2 requirement.

4. Verify locally:
   - replication/scripts/launch_jupyter.sh --port 8889 launches a
     working JupyterLab.
   - systemctl --user status while it's running shows MemoryMax=48G.

Testing notes
-------------
- Manual smoke: launch via the wrapper, open a trivial notebook,
  confirm a kernel runs.
- cgroup v2 guard: temporarily simulate failure by running with
  CGROUP_OVERRIDE=cgroup or similar, OR confirm the script bails
  with a clear error if /sys/fs/cgroup were ever not cgroup2fs.
- Memory-cap verification: in a notebook cell, deliberately allocate
  bytearray(60 * 1024**3). Expect the kernel to die with OOM, but the
  host to remain responsive (no freeze, no need to hard-reboot).
  Document the observation in the PR.
- No automated tests required — this is a launch script.

Acceptance
----------
- Wrapper script exists, executable, documented in
  replication/README.md.
- cgroup v2 guard is in place and produces a clear error on a v1
  host.
- Manually verified that an over-memory cell kills the kernel only,
  not the host.

title: wz1.2: Refactor cell 29 of ripser notebook (fork to spawn, parent-load to child-mmap)
description:
Summary
-------
The ripser notebook's barcode loop (cell 29) currently OOMs north because
the parent kernel pre-loads the full ~4.7 GB attention tensor via
np.load(filename, allow_pickle=True), then split_matricies_and_lengths
builds 20 fancy-indexed COPIES (~4.7 GB more), totaling ~9.4 GB resident
before any forks. Each fork()ed child inherits this COW.

Fix: drop the parent-side pre-load and the fancy-index copy entirely.
Keep the existing fork()-based multiprocessing — the OOM is parent
bloat, not fork inheritance. Pass (filename, indices, ...) to each
child instead of pre-loaded array slices. The child does
np.load(filename, mmap_mode='r')[indices] so only its slice ever
materializes in resident memory. Fork-COW shares the parent's mmap
page table cleanly (read-only mappings, no per-child duplication).

Why fork, not spawn: spawn-context Process re-imports __main__ to
unpickle the target function. In a Jupyter kernel __main__ has no
source file backing it, so spawn raises AttributeError on the
worker functions. Reviewer confirmed experimentally. Fork avoids
this problem entirely; the only thing fork was buying us as a
problem (page inheritance from a bloated parent) goes away once we
remove the parent-side bloat.

Notebook-faithfulness: keep function names (subprocess_wrap,
get_only_barcodes, format_barcodes, save_barcodes, unite_barcodes,
split_matricies_and_lengths) intact. Cell 27 is UNTOUCHED. Only
cell 28 (get_only_barcodes signature/body) and cell 29 (the loop)
change. Patches go through replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py
per the established RIPSER_PATCHES pattern — do NOT hand-edit the
notebook directly.

Files to modify
---------------
- replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py (extend RIPSER_PATCHES with
  two new entries for cells 28 and 29)
- replication/notebooks/features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb
  (regenerated by re-running the patch script after edits)

Files to create
---------------
- replication/tests/test_ripser_mmap.py (pytest verifying parent RSS
  stays bounded while a fork()ed child loads its slice via mmap)

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py (existing RIPSER_PATCHES
  pattern; cell-28 and cell-29 patches will extend this list)
- replication/notebooks/features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb
  (cells 28 and 29 — the current implementation to be replaced)
- reference/ripser_count.py (the get_barcodes API the child function
  calls; FROZEN — do not edit)

Implementation steps
--------------------
0. **Source the OLD strings via nbformat, not by typing them.** Run

       import nbformat
       nb = nbformat.read(
           "replication/notebooks/features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb",
           as_version=4,
       )
       print(repr(nb.cells[28].source))   # for the cell-28 patch
       print(repr(nb.cells[29].source))   # for the cell-29 patch

   and use the printed strings verbatim as your OLD constants in
   patch_notebook.py. The OLD blocks shown in this issue are for
   REVIEW ONLY — they may have whitespace drift (trailing spaces,
   indented "blank" lines) relative to the live notebook. If your
   patch_notebook.py run reports "patched" but the notebook diff is
   empty, you likely have a whitespace mismatch in OLD.

1. In replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py, define two new (old, new)
   patch pairs and append to RIPSER_PATCHES.

   Cell 28 patch — replace ONLY the get_only_barcodes definition
   (leave format_barcodes, save_barcodes, unite_barcodes,
   split_matricies_and_lengths in cell 28 untouched). The (old, new)
   string pair targets only the function block:

   OLD:
       def get_only_barcodes(adj_matricies, ntokens_array, dim, lower_bound):
           """Get barcodes from adj matricies for each layer, head"""
           barcodes = {}
           layers, heads = range(adj_matricies.shape[1]), range(adj_matricies.shape[2])
           for (layer, head) in itertools.product(layers, heads):
               matricies = adj_matricies[:, layer, head, :, :]
               barcodes[(layer, head)] = ripser_count.get_barcodes(matricies, ntokens_array, dim, lower_bound, (layer, head))
           return barcodes

   NEW:
       def get_only_barcodes(filename, indices, ntokens_array, dim, lower_bound):
           """Get barcodes from a slice of an attention .npy. Loads via
           mmap so the child process never materializes more than its
           slice; parent never materializes the array body at all."""
           adj_matricies = np.load(filename, mmap_mode='r')[indices]
           barcodes = {}
           layers, heads = range(adj_matricies.shape[1]), range(adj_matricies.shape[2])
           for (layer, head) in itertools.product(layers, heads):
               matricies = adj_matricies[:, layer, head, :, :]
               barcodes[(layer, head)] = ripser_count.get_barcodes(
                   matricies, ntokens_array, dim, lower_bound, (layer, head))
           return barcodes

   Cell 29 patch — replace the WHOLE cell source. The (old, new)
   pair takes the entire current cell body as old and the new
   indices-only loop as new.

   OLD (whole cell):
       queue = Queue()
       number_of_splits = 20
       for i, filename in enumerate(tqdm(adj_filenames, desc='Calculating barcodes')):
           barcodes = defaultdict(list)
           adj_matricies = np.load(filename, allow_pickle=True) # samples X
           print(f"Matricies loaded from: {filename}")
           ntokens = ntokens_array[i*batch_size*DUMP_SIZE : (i+1)*batch_size*DUMP_SIZE]
           splitted = split_matricies_and_lengths(adj_matricies, ntokens, number_of_splits)
           for matricies, ntokens in tqdm(splitted, leave=False):
               p = Process(
                   target=subprocess_wrap,
                   args=(
                       queue,
                       get_only_barcodes,
                       (matricies, ntokens, dim, lower_bound)
                   )
               )
               p.start()
               barcodes_part = queue.get() # block until putted and get barcodes from the queue
       #         print("Features got.")
               p.join() # release resources
       #         print("The process is joined.")
               p.close() # releasing resources of ripser
       #         print("The proccess is closed.")

               barcodes = unite_barcodes(barcodes, barcodes_part)
           part = filename.split('_')[-1].split('.')[0]
           save_barcodes(barcodes, barcodes_file + '_' + part + '.json')

   NEW (whole cell):
       queue = Queue()
       number_of_splits = 20
       for i, filename in enumerate(tqdm(adj_filenames, desc='Calculating barcodes')):
           barcodes = defaultdict(list)
           print(f"Processing: {filename}")
           ntokens = ntokens_array[i*batch_size*DUMP_SIZE : (i+1)*batch_size*DUMP_SIZE]
           splitted_ids = np.array_split(np.arange(len(ntokens)), number_of_splits)
           for indices in tqdm(splitted_ids, leave=False):
               p = Process(
                   target=subprocess_wrap,
                   args=(
                       queue,
                       get_only_barcodes,
                       (filename, indices, ntokens[indices], dim, lower_bound)
                   )
               )
               p.start()
               barcodes_part = queue.get()
               p.join()
               p.close()
               barcodes = unite_barcodes(barcodes, barcodes_part)
           part = filename.split('_')[-1].split('.')[0]
           save_barcodes(barcodes, barcodes_file + '_' + part + '.json')

   Idempotency check before merging: confirm NEW does not contain OLD
   as a substring (it doesn't — adj_matricies = np.load... is gone,
   split_matricies_and_lengths is gone). Patch script's substring-
   replace approach stays idempotent.

2. Run python replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py --notebook
   replication/notebooks/features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb.
   Verify it reports "patched" the first time and "already patched
   (no-op)" on the second.

3. Add replication/tests/test_ripser_mmap.py:
   - Write a tiny attention .npy fixture (e.g. 8 samples × 2 layers ×
     2 heads × 8 × 8 fp16) to a tmp path. Use np.save.
   - Capture parent baseline RSS via psutil.Process().memory_info().rss
     BEFORE doing anything that touches the file.
   - Define a worker function (in a real .py file, not a notebook) that
     does np.load(path, mmap_mode='r')[indices] and returns checksum.
   - Spawn one child via the default Process (fork on Linux); have it
     run the worker on indices=[1,2,3]; assert the checksum is correct.
   - Assert parent RSS after the child returns has grown by < 10 MB
     (the mmap page table is shared; no array body is materialized in
     the parent). Use a small fudge factor; the array body is 8KB per
     fixture, so headroom is generous.
   - Assert mp.get_start_method(allow_none=True) is None or "fork"
     (NOT "spawn") — guards against a future regression that sets
     mp.set_start_method("spawn") globally and breaks Jupyter
     execution.

4. Manual smoke run: open the patched notebook in Jupyter (under
   wz1.1's launcher, so a kernel OOM is recoverable), run cells up
   through cell 29 on a tiny fixture or on the smallest available
   attention .npy. Confirm parent RSS via top stays bounded and
   barcodes JSON gets written.

Testing notes
-------------
This is the LOAD-BEARING fix for the OOM. The pytest test in step 3
is the firewall. Two assertions matter:

a. Start method is NOT spawn. Reason: spawn-context Process re-imports
   __main__ to unpickle target/args. In Jupyter __main__ has no source
   file, so spawn breaks the notebook. We must stay on fork to keep
   the notebook-faithful pattern working.

b. Parent RSS stays bounded across a child run. Reason: this verifies
   we genuinely removed the parent pre-load. If someone re-introduces
   np.load(..., allow_pickle=True) in cell 29, this test catches it.

Other checks:
- python -m py_compile replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py after
  edits.
- Patch script idempotency: re-running on an already-patched notebook
  reports no-op.
